verses.text: The children of Israel again did evil in the sight of the LORD, when Ehud was dead. The LORD sold them into the hand of Jabin king of Canaan, that reigned in Hazor; the captain of whose host was Sisera, which dwelt in Harosheth of the Gentiles. The children of Israel cried unto the LORD: for he had nine hundred chariots of iron; and twenty years he mightily oppressed the children of Israel. Guidance: 'When Ehud was dead' — eighty years of peace, then immediately back into evil. Faith does not run in the blood; each generation must choose. Jabin's name and city (Hazor) deliberately echo the king Joshua burned in Josh 11; the burned dynasty has rebuilt. Israel never followed up on the win. The LORD's discipline is precise — 900 iron chariots is exactly the technology Israel feared in Josh 17:18 and refused to face by faith. The thing avoided becomes the thing that comes to find you.

Deborah, a prophetess, the wife of Lapidoth, she judged Israel at that time. She dwelt under the palm tree of Deborah between Ramah and Beth-el in mount Ephraim: and the children of Israel came up to her for judgment. Guidance: A prophetess and a judge. Two firsts in one verse — Israel's only female judge in the book, and the only judge explicitly named a prophet. The other judges fight first and rule second; Deborah rules first and is so trusted that men come to her tree for verdicts. The palm tree (not a courtroom, not a city gate) is her bench. Leadership is what people seek out, regardless of where it sits.

She dwelt under the palm tree of Deborah between Ramah and Beth-el in mount Ephraim: and the children of Israel came up to her for judgment. Guidance: A prophetess and a judge. Two firsts in one verse — Israel's only female judge in the book, and the only judge explicitly named a prophet. The other judges fight first and rule second; Deborah rules first and is so trusted that men come to her tree for verdicts. The palm tree (not a courtroom, not a city gate) is her bench. Leadership is what people seek out, regardless of where it sits.

She sent and called Barak son of Abinoam out of Kedesh-naphtali, and said unto him, Hath not the LORD God of Israel commanded, saying, Go and draw toward mount Tabor, and take with thee ten thousand men of the children of Naphtali and of the children of Zebulun? I will draw unto thee to the river Kishon Sisera, the captain of Jabin's army, with his chariots and his multitude; and I will deliver him into thine hand. Guidance: 'Hath not the LORD commanded?' — Deborah is repeating an order Barak should already know about. The strategy is divine: muster on Mount Tabor (a steep wooded hill chariots can't climb), and the LORD will lure Sisera's army to the Kishon valley below. The 'I' in 'I will draw' is the LORD's. Even the plan to set the enemy up is His. Deborah is not the strategist; she is the messenger of a strategy already drawn in heaven.

She sent and called Barak son of Abinoam out of Kedesh-naphtali, and said unto him, Hath not the LORD God of Israel commanded, saying, Go and draw toward mount Tabor, and take with thee ten thousand men of the children of Naphtali and of the children of Zebulun? I will draw unto thee to the river Kishon Sisera, the captain of Jabin's army, with his chariots and his multitude; and I will deliver him into thine hand. Guidance: 'Hath not the LORD commanded?' — Deborah is repeating an order Barak should already know about. The strategy is divine: muster on Mount Tabor (a steep wooded hill chariots can't climb), and the LORD will lure Sisera's army to the Kishon valley below. The 'I' in 'I will draw' is the LORD's. Even the plan to set the enemy up is His. Deborah is not the strategist; she is the messenger of a strategy already drawn in heaven.

Barak said unto her, If thou wilt go with me, then I will go: but if thou wilt not go with me, then I will not go. She said, I will surely go with thee: notwithstanding the journey that thou takest shall not be for thine honour; for the LORD shall sell Sisera into the hand of a woman. Deborah arose, and went with Barak to Kedesh. Guidance: Hebrews 11:32 will name Barak among the heroes of faith — but in this chapter he is the man who hedges. He believes God's promise, but only if Deborah comes too. She agrees without complaint, but speaks a prophetic footnote: a woman will get the credit. He thinks she means herself; the chapter will reveal she means Jael. Half-trust does not lose the victory, but it can redirect the glory. The LORD will still save Israel; the headline just won't carry Barak's name.

Deborah arose, and went with Barak to Kedesh. Guidance: Hebrews 11:32 will name Barak among the heroes of faith — but in this chapter he is the man who hedges. He believes God's promise, but only if Deborah comes too. She agrees without complaint, but speaks a prophetic footnote: a woman will get the credit. Barak called Zebulun and Naphtali to Kedesh; and he went up with ten thousand men at his feet: and Deborah went up with him. Now Heber the Kenite, which was of the children of Hobab the father in law of Moses, had severed himself from the Kenites, and pitched his tent unto the plain of Zaanaim, which is by Kedesh. Guidance: 'Ten thousand men at his feet' — and Deborah at his side. Then the narrator inserts a single sentence about Heber the Kenite. It looks like a digression. It isn't. The narrator is laying the chess pieces for the chapter's last move. Heber's wife Jael will turn out to be the woman Deborah prophesied about. The book of Judges teaches its readers to notice the small mentions; they are the seeds the harvest will need.

They shewed Sisera that Barak son of Abinoam was gone up to mount Tabor. Sisera gathered together all his chariots, even nine hundred chariots of iron, and all the people that were with him, from Harosheth of the Gentiles unto the river of Kishon. Guidance: Sisera takes the bait Deborah described. Nine hundred chariots roll into the Kishon valley — the very killing ground God had picked. The army that should be the most terrified for the most years now charges to the trap. Pride hurries; God waits at the river.

They shewed Sisera that Barak son of Abinoam was gone up to mount Tabor. Sisera gathered together all his chariots, even nine hundred chariots of iron, and all the people that were with him, from Harosheth of the Gentiles unto the river of Kishon. Guidance: Sisera takes the bait Deborah described. Nine hundred chariots roll into the Kishon valley — the very killing ground God had picked. The army that should be the most terrified for the most years now charges to the trap. Pride hurries; God waits at the river.

Deborah said unto Barak, Up; for this is the day in which the LORD hath delivered Sisera into thine hand: is not the LORD gone out before thee? So Barak went down from mount Tabor, and ten thousand men after him. The LORD discomfited Sisera, and all his chariots, and all his host, with the edge of the sword before Barak; so that Sisera lighted down off his chariot, and fled away on his feet. Barak pursued after the chariots, and after the host, unto Harosheth of the Gentiles: and all the host of Sisera fell upon the edge of the sword; and there was not a man left. Guidance: Deborah's whole speech is six words: 'Up; for this is the day.' She does not strategize; she announces. Judges 5:21 will reveal what the prose understates here — the Kishon river flooded, the chariots got stuck, and God turned an iron fleet into mud-locked junk. The general dismounts and runs. The army that terrified Israel for twenty years dies in a single afternoon. The LORD's promise to deliver the chariots was kept by His own weather. Faith waits for the cue; God supplies the storm.

Howbeit Sisera fled away on his feet to the tent of Jael the wife of Heber the Kenite: for there was peace between Jabin the king of Hazor and the house of Heber the Kenite. Jael went out to meet Sisera, and said unto him, Turn in, my lord, turn in to me; fear not. When he had turned in unto her into the tent, she covered him with a mantle. Guidance: Sisera flees to what he thinks is a safe house. Heber's clan has a peace treaty with Hazor; Jael is on his side, politically. She greets him with the exact words enemies dread: 'Fear not.' The first three Hebrew words out of her mouth are reassurance. He is exhausted, she is hospitable, the cover seems normal. He has no reason to think it strange — except that he is a foreign general hiding in the women's tent (which custom said no man not her husband could enter). The very space that should have warned him is what comforts him.

Howbeit Sisera fled away on his feet to the tent of Jael the wife of Heber the Kenite: for there was peace between Jabin the king of Hazor and the house of Heber the Kenite. Jael went out to meet Sisera, and said unto him, Turn in, my lord, turn in to me; fear not. When he had turned in unto her into the tent, she covered him with a mantle. Guidance: Sisera flees to what he thinks is a safe house. Heber's clan has a peace treaty with Hazor; Jael is on his side, politically. She greets him with the exact words enemies dread: 'Fear not.' The first three Hebrew words out of her mouth are reassurance. He is exhausted, she is hospitable, the cover seems normal. He has no reason to think it strange — except that he is a foreign general hiding in the women's tent (which custom said no man not her husband could enter). The very space that should have warned him is what comforts him.

He said unto her, Give me, I pray thee, a little water to drink; for I am thirsty. She opened a bottle of milk, and gave him drink, and covered him. Again he said unto her, Stand in the door of the tent, and it shall be, when any man doth come and inquire of thee, and say, Is there any man here? that thou shalt say, No. Guidance: He asks for water; she gives him milk — richer, heavier, the kind of drink that sends a tired man to sleep. She covers him a second time. He posts her as his lookout: lie if anyone asks. Then he closes his eyes. The hospitality is gentle; the betrayal is total. Some attack with iron chariots; Jael attacks with milk and a blanket. The chapter's quiet warning is that the deepest dangers may not feel like danger at all.

He said unto her, Give me, I pray thee, a little water to drink; for I am thirsty. She opened a bottle of milk, and gave him drink, and covered him. Again he said unto her, Stand in the door of the tent, and it shall be, when any man doth come and inquire of thee, and say, Is there any man here? that thou shalt say, No. Guidance: He asks for water; she gives him milk — richer, heavier, the kind of drink that sends a tired man to sleep. She covers him a second time. He posts her as his lookout: lie if anyone asks. Then he closes his eyes. The hospitality is gentle; the betrayal is total. Some attack with iron chariots; Jael attacks with milk and a blanket. The chapter's quiet warning is that the deepest dangers may not feel like danger at all.

Then Jael Heber's wife took a nail of the tent, and took an hammer in her hand, and went softly unto him, and smote the nail into his temples, and fastened it into the ground: for he was fast asleep and weary. So he died. And, behold, as Barak pursued Sisera, Jael came out to meet him, and said unto him, Come, and I will shew thee the man whom thou seekest. When he came into her tent, behold, Sisera lay dead, and the nail was in his temples. Guidance: Jael uses what is at hand. Nomadic women routinely pitched the tents; she handled tent pegs daily. The everyday tool becomes the war hammer. 'Went softly' — three words that capture the whole sequence of betrayal. Then she steps outside, calmly, and shows Barak the dead general she's just executed in her own tent. Deborah's prophecy comes true word for word: 'the LORD shall sell Sisera into the hand of a woman.' The conqueror of Israel is brought down not on a battlefield but on a tent floor, by a woman whose husband is on the wrong political side, with the most ordinary thing in her toolbox.

So God subdued on that day Jabin the king of Canaan before the children of Israel. The hand of the children of Israel prospered, and prevailed against Jabin the king of Canaan, until they had destroyed Jabin king of Canaan. Guidance: The narrator's verdict: 'God subdued.' Two prose verses condense the rest of the campaign. Jabin's name is repeated four times in two verses — the dynasty Joshua thought he had ended in chapter 11 is finally finished here. The text is patient with God's slow finishing. He completes what humans only start.
